coredata.dc:title: Sensitive electrochemical sensing of imidacloprid based on mulberry-like gold-graphene quantum dots hybird as electrode material
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Dan
Li, Nana
Zhu, Min
Dai, Weichen
coredata.dc:description: The rapid synthesis of gold nanocrystal with high catalytic activity is still a great challenge to improve the analytical performance of electrochemical sensors. In this paper, gold-arginine-threonine functionalized graphene quantum dot hybrid (Au-Arg-Thr-GQD) was synthesized by a simple and fast one-step method using Arg-Thr-GQD as reductant, stabilizer and crosslinker. The Au-Arg-Thr-GQD offers mulberry-shaped structure by the interconnection and self-assembly of Arg-Thr-GQD and small-sized Au nanocrystals. The unique structure significantly improves the catalytic activity of hybrid. Au-Arg-Thr-GQD was used as a sensing material for electrochemical analysis of imidacloprid. Under the optimal conditions, the differential pulse voltammetry peak current linearly increased with the increasing of imidacloprid concentration in the range of 0.1–20 μM with the detection limit of 0.011 μM. The proposed imidacloprid sensor shows high selectivity, good stability and reproducibility, and has been successfully applied to the determination of imidacloprid in cabbage and cucumber samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mulberry-like gold nanomaterial
Graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical sensor
Imidacloprid

Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of Jiangsu Province, Grant/Award Number: 202113981012Y.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image (A-B), TEM image (C), XRD pattern (D), Raman spectrum (E) and FTIR spectrum (F) of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD.
The CV and EIS curves of bare GCE (a), Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE (b), Au/GCE (C) and Au-Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE (d) in the PBS of 7.0 containing 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- at the scan rate of 100 mV s−1.
A: DPV curves of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE in different pH PBS from 4.0 to 9.0 (from right to left) containing 4 μM IMI. B: The relationship curves of peak potential, peak current with the pH value.
A: The DPV curves of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE in the pH 7.0 PBS containing different concentrations of IMI. B: The relationship curve between the DPV peak current and IMI concentration.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor and its electrochemical reduction of IMI.
The proposed mechanism of the electrocatalytic reduction of IMI.
Comparison of the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE for detection of IMI with other modified electrodes.
The results for detection of IMI in cabbage and cucumber samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Sensitive electrochemical sensing of imidacloprid based on mulberry-like gold-graphene quantum dots hybird as electrode material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine Hanlin College, Taizhou 225300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine Hanlin College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, Nanjing University of Chinese Medicine Hanlin College, Taizhou 225300, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid synthesis of gold nanocrystal with high catalytic activity is still a great challenge to improve the analytical performance of electrochemical sensors. In this paper, gold-arginine-threonine functionalized graphene quantum dot hybrid (Au-Arg-Thr-GQD) was synthesized by a simple and fast one-step method using Arg-Thr-GQD as reductant, stabilizer and crosslinker. The Au-Arg-Thr-GQD offers mulberry-shaped structure by the interconnection and self-assembly of Arg-Thr-GQD and small-sized Au nanocrystals. The unique structure significantly improves the catalytic activity of hybrid. Au-Arg-Thr-GQD was used as a sensing material for electrochemical analysis of imidacloprid. Under the optimal conditions, the differential pulse voltammetry peak current linearly increased with the increasing of imidacloprid concentration in the range of 0.1–20 μM with the detection limit of 0.011 μM. The proposed imidacloprid sensor shows high selectivity, good stability and reproducibility, and has been successfully applied to the determination of imidacloprid in cabbage and cucumber samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Date will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Imidacloprid (IMI) is the world's largest sales of neonicotinoid insecticide, mainly used to control rice, wheat, vegetables and other stinging insect pests [1]. IMI kills insects by binding to nicotinic acetylcholine receptors that cause overstimulation and paralysis. It has the characteristics of high water solubility, high persistence, high stability and strong bioeffects [2,3]. Low utilization rate leads to most of IMI remaining in crops, soil and water, causing a series of problems such as food safety, environmental pollution, colony collapse disease and male reproductive toxicity [4–6]. IMI has been classified as a Class II toxic substance by the World Health Organization [7]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a fast and effective method for the determination of IMI. At present, the main analytical methods for the determination of IMI are high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [9] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [10]. However, these methods often require complete laboratories, expensive instruments and skilled operators [11,12], so it is difficult to achieve rapid detection and on-site analysis of IMI. In recent years, electrochemical sensors have received more attention in the detection of agricultural residues due to its high sensitivity, fast response speed and low cost, making it possible to achieve field work [13].

The catalytic performance of electrode materials is one of the main factors that affect the analytical performance of sensors. Gold nanoparticles have been widely used as sensing materials due to their high electron transfer ability, excellent electrocatalytic activity and good biocompatibility [14,15]. In order to improve the catalytic activity and stability of gold nanoparticles to meet the practical application of electrochemical sensors, many efforts have been made to synthesize gold nanomaterials. Er et al. reported the seed-mediated method [16]. The gold nanoparticles were prepared using gold seed solution obtained by adding sodium borohydride to a solution containing HAuCl4 and hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide. Gold nanoparticles and reduced graphene modified screen-printed electrode was used for the electrochemical determination of topotecan. The results showed a wide linear range of 0.1–16.0 μM and a low detection limit of 22.0 nM. However, the preparation process of this method is complex, and the yield is low. Chen et al. reported the microwave-assisted synthesis of Au@Ni-MOFs [17]. Au nanoparticles were deposited on Ni-MOFs by fast microwave-assisted process, which speeds up the rate of charge transfer. The sensor based on Au@Ni-MOFs shows a wide detection range of 5–7400 μM and high sensitivity of 1447.1 μA mM−1 cm−2 for glucose. Duan et al. synthesized Au/ZnO by magnetron sputtering technique [18]. The prepared gold nanoparticles exhibit unique three-dimensional structure and good dispersion, which significantly improves the response signal of Au/ZnO sensors to nitrite. However, this method requires specialized equipment. Sabzehmeidani et al. proposed the chemical precipitation method preparation of Au-Pd-CoFe2O4 composite [19]. By chemical precipitation, Au–Pd nanoparticles were embedded on CoFe2O4. The prepared Au-Pd-CoFe2O4 showed superior ethanol oxidation activity. Zhong et al. reported an in-situ growth method for the synthesis of AuNPs/Zn-MOF [20]. The introduction of gold nanoparticles greatly improves the electrochemical active surface area of the composite. The AuNPs/Zn-MOF was applied to the construction of electrochemiluminescence sensor for the determination of miRNA-522. The sensor exhibits high sensitivity and low detection limit. However, sodium borohydride is often used as a reducing agent in the synthesis process. Sodium borohydride can increase the agglomeration of gold nanoparticles, which leads to the decrease of catalytic activity [21]. Hong et al. reported one-step sonochemical approach for the synthesis of Au-ferrocene-WS2 hybrid [22]. Au nanoparticles were assembled on WS2, which improves the electronic properties and water solubility of the hybrid. An electrochemical immunosensor based on the hybrid was constructed to realize the highly sensitive determination of carbohydrate antigen 72-4. Although many efforts have been made in the synthesis of gold nanomaterials, there are still great challenges in preparing gold nanomaterials with high catalytic activity.

In this work, we reported synthesis of gold-arginine-threonine functionalized graphene quantum dot hybrid (Au-Arg-Thr-GQD) with mulberry-like structure. The hybird showed high electrocatalytic activity. An electrochemical sensor based on the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD was constructed for the determination of IMI, and the results showed high sensitivity, good selectivity and stability.

Arginine (Arg), threonine (Thr), citric acid, chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), chitosan (CS), potassiumchloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), calciumchloride(CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium ferricyanide (K3(FeCN)6) and potassium ferrocyanide (K4(FeCN)6·3H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. Imidacloprid (IMI), carbendazim (Car), parathion (Par), acetamiprid (Ace) and phoxim (Pho) were purchased from Shanghai Pesticide Research Institute. Phosphate-buffered solution (PBS) was prepared from 0.1 M Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4 and KCl. All the solutions involved in the experiment were prepared with doubly distilled water.

The Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded on HITACHI S4800 scanning electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were recorded on JEOL 2010 FEG transmission electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on D8 Advance Instrument operated at 40 kV and 20 mA and using Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15406 nm). Infrared spectrum (IR) was measured on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer.

The Arg-Thr-GQD was synthesized by mixing citric acid (0.1 mol), Arg (0.1 mol) and Thr (0.2 mol) and then heating at 180 ℃ for 3 h. Arg-Thr-GQD (50 mg) was dissolved in doubly distilled water (10 mL) to form a uniform dispersion, then heated to 95 ℃. Under constant agitation, 1.3 mL HAuCl4 solution (10 mM) was rapidly added into the above solution and reacted for 2 min. Next, the resulting suspension was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 5 min. The obtained solids are re-dispersed in double-distilled water and stored in the refrigerator. Gold nanoparticles were prepared by direct reduction method using NaBH4 as reducing agent [23].

5 μL of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD solution was dropped on the treated glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface and dried at room temperature. Scheme 1 shows the preparation process of the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor and its electrochemical reduction of IMI. Au/GCE and Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE were prepared using the same method. The prepared electrodes were stored at 4℃ when not in use.

Electrochemical experiments were carried out on a CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instrument Co., China) with a three-electrode system at room temperature. The potential scanning range of cyclic voltammetry (CV) was set from −1.3 V to −0.5 V, and the scanning speed was 100 mV s−1. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in the frequency range of 1 Hz–100 KHz with a potential amplitude of ± 5 mV. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) test parameters were set as follows: a potential amplitude of 50 mV and a pulse width of 50 ms.

The synthesis of the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD hybrid was completed by a simple and fast one-step method. As shown in Fig. 1 , when HAuCl4 solution was added to Arg-Thr-GQD solution, the system rapidly changed from yellow to red within 2 min, accompanied by precipitation. In the whole process, Arg-Thr-GQD acts as a reductant. Arg-Thr-GQD with multiple reducing groups (–OH, –NH2) shows strong reducing ability, which can rapidly reduce Au3+ to Au0, and finally form burgundy gold nanoparticles. To confirm this suggestion, the spectral behavior of Arg-Thr-GQD and Au-Arg-Thr-GQD was determined by ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Fig. S1). Compared with Arg-Thr-GQD, the absorption spectrum of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD has a wider absorption peak at wavelength 530 nm, which is due to the surface plasmon resonance of the Au nanoparticles. It proves that the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD has been synthesized successfully. Secondly, Arg-Thr-GQD also plays the role of stabilizer. The Arg-Thr-GQDs were fixed on the surface of Au nanoparticles through the strong force between the hydrophilic groups (–OH, –NH2 and –COOH) and Au, which not only improves the stability of Au nanoparticles, but also improves their water solubility. Most importantly, Arg-Thr-GQD has multiple ligands, which can bind to different Au nanoparticles through chemical bonds, resulting in the Au nanoparticles to interconnect with each other and self-assembly to form 3D conductive network structures. This unique structure helps to improve electron transport, resulting in high catalytic activity.

The synthesized Au-Arg-Thr-GQD hybird was characterized by SEM, TEM, High resolution TEM (HRTEM), XRD and FTIR techniques. As can be seen from Fig. 2 , Au-Arg-Thr-GQD exhibits a mulberry-like structure with abundant pores, which is formed by the interconnection and self-assembly between Arg-Thr-GQD and multiple gold nanoparticles. The average size of Au nanoparticles is 8–13 nm. The unique structure is conducive to improving the electron/electrolyte transfer speed and increasing the electrocatalytic active area, so as to bring high catalytic activity. The 0.23 nm interplanar spacing that appears in the HRTEM image corresponds to the (111) plane of the Au nanoparticle. There are four diffraction peaks of 38.3°, 44.5°, 64.9° and 77.8° on the XRD pattern, corresponding to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) faces of the Au crystal respectively. On the Raman spectrum, the characteristic peaks at 1348 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1 are D-band and G-band of the Arg-Thr-GQD respectively. The D band is related to the presence of sp3 defect, and the G band corresponds to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon. On the FTIR spectrum, the peak at 3432 cm−1 is due to the symmetric vibration of N-H bond. The peak at 2960 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the C-H bond. The peak at 1698 cm−1 is the IR absorption produced by stretching vibration of the C=O bond. The absorption peaks at 1630 cm−1, 1398 cm−1 and 1046 cm−1 correspond to the absorption peak of C–O and N–H, -N–C=O, C–C bond. The above results prove that Au-Arg-Thr-GQD has been successfully prepared.

In order to evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD, the CV and EIS behaviors of Arg-Thr-GQD, Au and Arg-Thr-GQD-Au-modified GCE in a PBS of pH 7.0 containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- at the scan rate of 100 mV s−1 were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 , bare GCE produced a pair of weak redox peak currents and a big charge-transfer resistance (Rct) value. When Arg-Thr-GQD was modified to the GCE, the peak current increased and the Rct value decreased. This is due to the improved conductivity of Arg-Thr-GQD. Au/GCE produced a larger peak current and a smaller Rct value compared with Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE. This is due to the high catalytic activity of gold nanoparticles. When Au was replaced by Au-Arg-Thr-GQD, the peak current was further increased and the Rct value was further decreased. This is attributed to the introduction of Arg-Thr-GQD, which allows the hybrid to have more electrocatalytic active sites, resulting in better electrochemical properties.

The electrochemical kinetic behavior of the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE was investigated by CV in a PBS of pH 7.0 containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. Fig. S2 A shows the CV curves of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE at different scan rates (ν). The peak current increases linearly with ν in the range of 20–200 mV s−1. In addition, the increase of ν leads to positive shift of oxidation peak potential and negative shift of reduction peak potential. Fig. S2B shows the relationship curves of peak currents with the square root of ν. The regression equations were Ipa (μA) = 183.85 ν1/2 (mV/s)1/2 + 3.48 (R2 = 0.9997), Ipc (μA) = −215.58 ν1/2 (mV/s)1/2 –0.86 (R2 = 0.9985). The results indicate that the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE surface is a diffusion-controlled process.

The constructed Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor was used for the analysis of IMI. Fig. S3 shows the CV curves and DPV curves of the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor in the pH 7.0 PBS in the absence and the presence with 3 μM IMI. The results showed that there was no redox peak on CV curve and DPV curve in PBS solution, indicating that the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD did not undergo redox reaction in the potential range of −1.3–−0.5 V, and it only acted as a catalyst to accelerate the reduction reaction of IMI on the electrode surface. When 3 μM IMI was added to the PBS solution, a reduction peak appeared on the CV curve, which was produced by the reduction of nitro in IMI molecules to hydroxylamine derivatives [24]. The whole reaction process involves four electrons transfer. The main possible reactions involved were as shown in Scheme 2
[25]. A reduction peak was also generated on the DPV curve, and the current signal generated by DPV is significantly stronger than that of CV. Therefore, DPV technology is selected for the determination of IMI.

In order to obtain the best performance of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE, the effect of the pH values of PBS on the electrochemical detection of IMI was investigated. Fig. 4 A shows the DPV response curves of the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD/GCE to 4 μM IMI in PBS solution at different pH values. With the increase of pH value, the peak current of DPV continues to increase. When pH value reaches 7.0, the peak current reaches the maximum. As the pH value continues to increase, the peak current begins to decrease. Therefore, pH 7.0 PBS was selected for the following experiments. Fig. 4B shows the relationship curves of peak potential, peak current with the pH value. With the increase of pH value, the reduction peak potential moves to the negative, and the reduction peak potential has a good linear relationship with pH value, the linear equation is: Epc = −0.0561 pH-0.5623, R2 = 0.995. The slope of the curve is −56.1 mV/pH, which is close to the theoretical value −59 mV/pH. The results show that the reduction process is an equal electron and proton.

The amount of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD modification is a very important factor in improving the electrocatalytic activity. The effect of the modified amount was studied. It can be observed from Fig. S4 that the reduction peak current increases gradually as the modified amount increases from 1 μL to 5 μL. As the modified amount continues to increase, the peak current begins to decrease. This is because a thicker layer of Au-Arg-Thr-GQD was formed on electrode surface, and the decrease of the electrochemically active area and conductivity of the electrode leads to a decrease in the peak current. Therefore, the optimal modified amount is 5 μL.

The reproducibility and stability of the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor were investigated. Ten Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensors were prepared by the same procedure for the determination of 3 μM IMI in pH 7.0 PBS. The RSD of the generated DPV peak current was 2.01 %, indicating that the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor has good repeatability. The prepared Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor was placed in the refrigerator (4 ℃) for 5 weeks, the DPV peak current still maintains 95.1 % of the initial value, indicating that the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor has high stability.

The selectivity of the Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor is a very important factor. For vegetable samples, common inorganic ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, NO3 –) and pesticides (carbendazim, parathion, acetamidine and phoxiphos) were selected as potential interferents. These potential interferents (80 μM) were mixed with 8 μM IMI, and the response signal of the mixture solution was determined by DPV. As shown in Fig. S5, the coexistence of inorganic ions and pesticides in the determination of IMI did not cause significant changes in the DPV peak current, indicating that the proposed Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor has high selectivity for the detection of IMI.

In order to investigate the practical application performance of the constructed Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor, the samples of vegetables (cabbage and cucumber) were selected as the objects, which were obtained from local markets. And the IMI in vegetables was determined by standard addition recovery method. The samples were processed according to the literature method [33]. Proper amount of cucumber and cabbage were crushed into pulp in homogenizer respectively. Then filter with 0.45 filter membrane. The obtained filtrate is diluted by PBS solution as the actual sample. The IMI standard solution (1, 5, 10 μM) was added to the prepared sample, and the IMI concentration was determined using the constructed Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor. Fig. S6 shows the DPV response curves at different concentrations in cabbage and cucumber samples. The results are shown in Table 2 , the recovery rate of IMI is between 97 and 104 %, and the RSD ranged from 2.2 to 3.4 %, indicating that the constructed sensor can be used for the determination of IMI in vegetables with high accuracy.

This paper describes the synthesis of mulberry-like Au-Arg-Thr-GQD hybrid and its use as a sensing platform for the electrochemical determination of IMI. The results show that the hybrid exhibit high electrocatalytic activity to IMI, which is attributed to the formation of a three-dimensional conductive network to accelerate the conduction of electrons and the synergistic effect between Au and Arg-Thr-GQD. The Au-Arg-Thr-GQD sensor has high sensitivity, wide linear range and good selectivity in the determination of IMI. The excellent electrochemical properties make Au-Arg-Thr-GQD one of the most promising sensing materials in the future.

Dan Xu: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization. Nana Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Min Zhu: Validation, Supervision. Weichen Dai: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from Jiangsu Higher Education program (23KJD150006), Science and Technology Support Program (Social Development) of Taizhou, Grant/Award Number: SSF20230038 and SSF20230029. Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship of Jiangsu Province, Grant/Award Number: 202113981012Y.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110377.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
